compressed_prompt: post on islamic history students' hajj story, lowercase, story twice
response:
a story of footsteps, not mine, but traced in books first. my hajj began in a library carrel, with the scent of dust and aging paper. i followed ibn battuta’s arduous route, felt the dread of pilgrims fearing bandits, the awe of finally seeing the minarets of mecca from a desert rise. i calculated the qibla with ancient astronomers, wept with rabia al-adawiyya’s longing. my pilgrimage was one of ink and longing, a map of the heart drawn before i ever bought a ticket.

a story of footsteps, now mine, echoing on the hot marble of masjid al-haram. the library’s map became real, overwhelming, a symphony of sobs and prayers in a hundred tongues. i saw the black stone, not as a historical artifact, but as a vortex of lived faith. i stood on arafat, and the words of all those scholars and poets i’d studied became a single, silent cry. my pilgrimage was one of sweat and submission, the map of the heart finally walked, step by trembling step.

the circle closed. the student of history became a part of it.
